description: Task 3: Adversarial robustness (AUROC) under manifest mimicry (MM), Runtime Trace Mimicry Attack (RTMA), and their composition (paper tab:adversarial). mean over seeds {7, 42, 123, 777, 2024}; std <= 0.006 for all cells. For RTMA, every baseline is re-scored on re-instrumented, re-featurized APKs (the timing shim is a bytecode modification).

protocol.manifest_mimicry: Greedy addition of low-risk permissions from the target category profile, retaining at least one functional high-risk permission; declared sets only.
protocol.rtma: Timing shim delaying permission API invocations to match the benign inter-arrival distribution of the same category (learned from 50 benign apps/category), suppressing no calls. Traces pass a per-application Pearson chi-square goodness-of-fit test vs the pooled category timing profile (12 log-spaced inter-arrival bins, alpha = 0.05; failure to reject).
protocol.composition: MM and RTMA applied to the same APK.

key_findings: Manifest-consuming methods are hit hardest by MM (DREBIN -19.3, MaMaDroid -18.2 AUROC points); code/graph-structural methods are largely insulated (DexRay -3.2, MaskDroid -2.1).
TrustGuard sits in between under MM (-7.2) because the declared-permission vector is one of four encoder inputs, diluted by description/code/graph channels MM cannot touch.
Under RTMA TrustGuard loses 11.6 points (0.963 -> 0.847) where install-time methods barely move (<= 0.9).
Under the composed attack TrustGuard retains 0.802 but its advantage over DexRay/MaskDroid inverts; the runtime-trace dependency is the composed attack's entry point.
Runtime governance's case rests on enforcement (task2_enforcement.json), not adversarial dominance.
